blurb: A tough native hawthorn of streambanks and foothill draws: clouds of white spring flowers, glossy leaves, and dark wine-black haws that feed the birds, all on a thorny small tree that makes superb wildlife cover. A caterpillar keystone — and it wants a bit more water than the xeric natives.
size: 10–20 ft tall × 8–15 ft wide (small tree / large shrub)
water: Moderate — a riparian native that wants more water than the xeric plants; not for dry zones
spread: Single- or multi-stemmed thorny small tree; can sucker into a thicket
seasons: Clusters of white flowers in spring; glossy toothed leaves in summer; dark purple-black haws in fall that persist; bare thorny structure in winter
wildlife: A caterpillar keystone (host to ~90 butterfly and moth species); spring nectar for bees; haws feed many birds and mammals; dense thorny cover and nesting
deer: Browsed when young; the thorns deter larger browsers
toxic: The ripe haws are edible (see Edible parts); the thorns are sharp and the seeds should not be eaten (cyanogenic, like other Rosaceae)
